title: Statement from Federal Reserve Bank of Cleveland President Beth Hammack regarding her vote at the Federal Open Market Committee’s April 28–29, 2026 meeting
summary: In this statement explaining her April 2026 FOMC vote, Hammack supported holding the funds rate steady but dissented from the post-meeting statement, objecting to its easing bias ('additional adjustments'). She views a clear easing bias as no longer appropriate given an outlook of broad-based inflation pressures and rising oil prices, even as she acknowledges two-sided risks (upside to inflation, downside to growth and employment). The dissent is hawkish: she wants forward guidance stripped of any signal of further cuts.

key_quotes.quote: I dissented from the post-meeting statement because I did not believe it was appropriate to include an easing bias around the future path for monetary policy.
key_quotes.context: Her hawkish dissent against forward guidance signaling further cuts

key_quotes.quote: I see this clear easing bias as no longer appropriate given the outlook.
key_quotes.context: Reasoning for opposing additional easing signals

key_quotes.quote: Inflation pressures continue to be broad based, and rising oil prices present an additional source of inflationary pressure.
key_quotes.context: Her read on inflation persistence and oil-driven upside

key_quotes.quote: Uncertainty around the economic outlook is elevated, with upside risks to inflation and downside risks to growth and employment.
key_quotes.context: Two-sided risk framing balancing the mandate
